headline: The S&P 500 Just Had Its Best Month Since 2020. Don’t Let ‘Sell in May’ Spook You.
summary: There was a lot that could have made the stock market fall.  Investors shrugged off questions about when—or even if—the Federal Reserve would cut interest rates again this year after the central bank held rates steady, and it ignored the fact that Jerome Powell plans to stay on the Fed’s Board of Governors even as Kevin Warsh prepares to become chair. Inflation came in hot as oil prices continued to rise, and earnings from  Microsoft  and  Meta Platforms  revealed sizable capital spending increases—and resulted in falling stocks.  Eli Lilly  earnings offered evidence that weight-loss drugs were only getting bigger;  Coca-Cola’s  beat showed that not all soft-drink makers are suffering from an Ozempic overhang;  Alphabet’s  capital spending paid off with big profits; and  Caterpillar’s  numbers demonstrated that there’s more to its business than mining and machinery.

headline: Should Capri Sun Hydrate’s Healthier Kids’ Drink Push Require Action From Kraft Heinz (KHC) Investors?
summary: In April 2026, Kraft Heinz expanded its Capri Sun portfolio with Capri Sun Hydrate, a kid-focused electrolyte drink with lower sugar, added Vitamin E, and no artificial ingredients, rolling out across major US retailers including Walmart, Target, and Amazon. This launch targets growing demand for healthier, functional kids’ beverages and showcases Kraft Heinz’s push to refresh legacy brands through innovation and new consumption occasions. We’ll examine how the Capri Sun Hydrate launch,...

headline: The Friday Checkout: Amazon says it’s the second-largest U.S. grocer — stores (mostly) not required
summary: The company’s grocery business brought in over $150 billion in gross sales last year, yet its brick-and-mortar presence relies mostly on its specialty chain, Whole Foods.

headline: This Tech Billionaire's Net Worth Crossed $300 Billion In April On AI Rally As Elon Musk Stays Richest In The World
summary: AI-driven tech stocks reshaped billionaire rankings, lifting Larry Page to $300B while Elon Musk stayed richest, according to Forbes.
